revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:平等寺 (桜井市)]]
{{Infobox Buddhist Temple | Name = Byodoji Temple | image = File:Byodo-ji (Sakurai), keidai.jpg | Location = 38 Miwa, Sakurai City, Nara Prefecture, Japan | Coordinates = {{Coord|34|31|36.3|N|135|51|14.8|E|region:JP-29_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Byodoji Temple|qq=}} | Mountain Name = [[Mount Miwa]] | Sect = [[Soto Zen Buddhism]] | Main Deity = [[Eleven-Faced Kannon]] | Established = Unknown | Founder = Traditionally attributed to [[Prince Shotoku]] | Formal Name = | Alternate Name = Miwa Bessho | Pilgrimage = [[Yamato Hokubu 88 Temples Pilgrimage]], 81st temple | Cultural Assets = | Map = {{Location map|Japan Nara|lat_deg=34|lat_min=31|lat_sec=36.3|lon_deg=135|lon_min=51|lon_sec=14.8|label=Byodoji|mark=Japanese Map symbol (Temple).svg|marksize=10|position=center|width=200|float=center|qq=}} | religious_affiliation= {{ill|Sōtō|qid=Q749085|1=Sōtō}} | prefecture= {{ill|Sakurai|qid=Q127026|1=Sakurai, Nara}} | coordinates= {{Coord|34.52675|N|135.854111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | founded_by= {{ill|Kyōen|qid=Q17216836|1=Kyōen}} | deity= {{ill|Eleven-Faced Avalokiteśvara|qid=Q11404731|1=Ekādaśamukha}} | part_of= {{ill|Q22120745|qid=Q22120745|1=Yamato Northern 88 Sacred Sites}} }} '''Byodoji Temple''' (平等寺, *Byodoji*) is a '''Soto Zen Buddhist temple''' located in '''Miwa, Sakurai City, Nara Prefecture, Japan'''. Its '''mountain name''' (*sangō*) is '''Mount Miwa''' (三輪山), and its '''main deity''' is '''Eleven-Faced Kannon''' (*Jūichimen Kannon*). The temple was formerly one of the '''Shingon Buddhist jinguji''' (shrine temples) associated with '''Miwa Myojin''', now known as '''Omiwa Shrine'''. It is also referred to as '''Miwa Bessho'''. == '''History''' According to tradition, '''Prince Shotoku''' (*Shōtoku Taishi*) founded the temple, and it was later revived by the monk '''Kyōen''' (*慶円*).<ref>“Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Nara Prefecture Place Names,” Heibonsha, 1981, p.435.</ref><ref>The Sakurai City official tourism guide states that it was established in *Bidatsu 10* (581 CE) by *Prince Shotoku*, but this is questionable as he would have been only eight years old at the time.</ref> The *Dai Miwa Chōshi* (*大三輪町史*) also suggests that before Byodoji, a temple called *Dai Miwaji Henjo-in* existed, which may have been founded by '''Kukai''' (*Kōbō Daishi*). Byodoji first appears clearly in historical records during the '''Kamakura period''', with the '''oldest mention in 1236 CE (Kanei 2nd year)''' in the text *Maitreya Nyorai Kanno-shō Sō*. According to this document, '''Kyōen''' established a '''Shingon Buddhist kanjō (esoteric initiation) hall''' near Miwa Shrine, referred to as '''Miwa Bessho'''. At that time, the temple was already known as Byodoji. From the '''late Kamakura period to the Meiji period''', Byodoji held the position of '''bettō-ji (head temple)''' of '''Miwa Myojin Shrine'''. Records in *Daijo-in Jisha Zasshiki* indicate that '''Kofukuji Temple''' imposed taxes on Byodoji, suggesting that it was one of Kofukuji’s subordinate temples, similar to many others in Yamato Province. Simultaneously, Byodoji also maintained ties with '''Daigo-ji Temple''', reflecting its involvement in '''Shugendo''' (*mountain asceticism*). As a result, Byodoji housed two different monastic groups: '''the Gakushu (scholars of Kofukuji's Daijo-in)''' and '''the Zenshu (practitioners of Daigo-ji's Sanbo-in)''', who coexisted within the temple. During the mid-Muromachi period, tensions escalated between these factions, as described in *Daijo-in Jisha Zasshiki*. By the '''Edo period''', Byodoji separated from Kofukuji and became a '''Shingon temple''' while still practicing Shugendo. It also served as a '''court prayer temple''' (*naiguji*), receiving '''80 koku''' of temple land grants. Historical records describe a '''large temple complex''' covering '''500m east-west and 330m north-south''', with various halls including: * '''Keien Shonin Founder's Hall''' * '''Gyoja-do (Hall of Ascetics)''' * '''Mieido (Portrait Hall)''' * '''Hondo (Main Hall)''' * '''Issaikyo-do (Sutra Repository)''' * '''12 sub-temples (坊舎)''' The '''former main temple site''' was located '''300m east of its current location''' in '''Miwa 1-ban'''. === '''Relation to the Shimazu Clan''' During the '''Battle of Sekigahara (1600 CE)''', '''Shimazu Yoshihiro''' and his '''80 surviving samurai''' escaped through enemy lines and took refuge at '''Byodoji'''. They stayed for '''70 days''', from '''September 15 to November 28''', and received financial assistance from the temple. In gratitude, they later donated '''40 koku of rice annually''' and constructed a '''three-room Goma Hall (護摩堂)'''. The Shimazu clan continued this tradition for '''300 years'''. === '''Meiji Period and Decline''' - In '''1868 (Meiji 1st year)''', the '''Shinbutsu Bunri (Separation of Shinto and Buddhism)''' policy led to the temple's suppression. - In '''1870 (Meiji 3rd year)''', Byodoji was confiscated and managed by Omiwa Shrine’s priests, leading to the destruction of its buildings. * '''1959 (Showa 34th year)''': The *Dai Miwa Chōshi* recorded that '''only the temple foundations remained'''. However, before the complete destruction, '''local supporters and former monks relocated key temple structures''' and '''protected its deities''', including: * '''The Eleven-Faced Kannon (Main Deity)''' * '''Miwa Fudo Myo-o''' * '''Statue of Kyōen''' * '''Buddha’s Footprint Stone (仏足石)''' The temple was '''revived as a Soto Zen Buddhist temple''' in '''1977 (Showa 52nd year)''' under the name '''"Miwayama Byodoji"'''. Restoration efforts were led by '''Monk Maruko Koho''', who spent '''16 years performing takuhatsu (alms-seeking)''' to fund temple reconstruction. === '''Modern Revival and the Shimazu Clan Connection''' - In '''1987 (Showa 62nd year)''', '''Shimazu descendants from Kagoshima''' visited Byodoji, where a '''memorial service''' was held for '''Shimazu Yoshihiro''' and his fallen warriors. - A '''biwa (Japanese lute) performance of “Ko-Atsumori”'''—a piece composed by '''Shimazu Yoshihiro'''—was dedicated at the temple. - In '''November 1987''', Byodoji’s head monk visited Kagoshima’s '''Fukusho-ji Temple''', the Shimazu family temple, to pay respects at the graves of their lords. == '''Temple Grounds''' * '''Main Hall (Zazen Hall)''' – Rebuilt in '''1987'''. Houses '''Eleven-Faced Kannon, Yakushi Nyorai, Amida Nyorai, Jizo Bosatsu, and Prince Shotoku’s statue'''.<ref name=":0">Confirmed via on-site signage.</ref> * '''Namikiri-do''' – Dedicated to '''Namikiri Fudo Myo-o'''.<ref name=":0" /> * '''Fudo-do''' – Houses '''Miwa Fudo Myo-o''', '''En no Gyoja''', and '''Rigen Daishi'''.<ref name=":0" /> * '''Sixteen Arhats Stone Statues''' * '''Two-Story Pagoda (Shaka Hall)''' – Rebuilt in '''2004 (Heisei 16th year)'''. * '''Akamon (Red Gate)''' – Hand-built by a former head monk in '''1976 (Showa 51st year)'''.<ref name=":0" /> * '''Inari Shrine''' – Dedicated to '''Fushimi Inari, Toyokawa Inari, and Magotaro Inari'''.<ref name=":0" /> * '''Shoro-do (Bell Tower)''' – Rebuilt in '''1987''', with a bell cast under the guidance of '''Living National Treasure Katori Masahiko'''.<ref name=":0" /> * '''Sanmon (Mountain Gate)''' – The '''only surviving structure''' from the original temple complex, facing the '''Yamanobe no Michi''' pilgrimage route. <gallery> File:Byodo-ji (Sakurai), Sanmon.jpg|Sanmon (Mountain Gate) File:Byodo-ji (Sakurai), keidai.jpg|View from the entrance File:Byodo-ji (Sakurai), Shoro-do.jpg|Bell Tower File:Byodo-ji (Sakurai), Nijyunoto.jpg|Two-Story Pagoda </gallery> == '''Related Temples''' * '''Yamato Hokubu 88 Temple Pilgrimage''' ** 80. '''Chogakuji''' → '''81. Byodoji''' → 82. '''Abe Monju-in''' {{Official website|https://www.byodoji.org|Byodoji Official Site|qq=}}
File:Byodo-ji (Sakurai), Sanmon.jpg|Sanmon (Mountain Gate) File:Byodo-ji (Sakurai
{{translated page|ja|平等寺 (桜井市)|version=101299735|comment=Imported full ja history}}
